data.title: Louisiana-Pacific: Fair Valuation For A High-Quality, Under-The-Radar Building Materials Giant
data.description:
Introduction

Louisiana-Pacific (LPX) is a rather boring manufacturing business at first glance, specializing in the production of building materials. Despite the fact that Warren Buffett owns around 8% of the company through Berkshire Hathaway, the company has attracted very little media attention in the past.

Since my last analysis on April 11, 2023 at a share price of $56.81, the company has outperformed the S&P 500 by ca. 16% with a total return of 68%. With this article, I would like to give an update on my original investment thesis.

To better understand Louisiana-Pacific, it can be broken down into three segments:

  • Siding – the hidden jewel of Louisiana-Pacific
  • OSB – a rather unattractive commodity business
  • South America – combines OSB and Siding in South American markets.

Since the segments differ significantly in their underlying economics, I believe it makes little sense to value the segments uniformly.

As a result, as I did back in 2023, I will evaluate the individual businesses with a sum of the parts analysis in the course of the analysis.

Siding

Let's start with the Siding business, which accounts for ca. 55% of total revenues. Within this segment, the company sells engineered wood products that are primarily used for lap, trim, fascia, soffit and sheds, but also in other smaller markets.

The share of revenue in this segment is 40% new construction, 40% reconstruction & repair (R&R) and 20% sheds and other ancillary markets.

Siding is a rapidly growing part of Louisiana-Pacific, which you can see in the numbers below:

Noteworthy is that:

  • Siding has achieved sales growth of + 7% p.a. and EBIT growth of + 11% p.a. since 2005, with an EBIT margin of 20% in FY 2024
  • Prices have not been lowered in any year, and have been raised on average by ca. 4% p.a.
  • The business operated with an ROIC of > 30%.

This is definitely a very good business! But why?

One of the main reasons is the structural advantages of engineered wood products compared to the substitutes vinyl, wood and fiber cement.

Over the past decade, engineered wood and fiber cement has gained significant market share from vinyl and wood siding. Wood has been losing market share for several decades because it simply cannot compete with vinyl, engineered wood and fiber cement (due to lower cost, easier installation and easier maintenance). Vinyl had its heyday in the mid-2000s and at that time had about 40% market share. Since then, engineered wood and fiber cement have become increasingly popular and have over time replaced vinyl. While vinyl has a right to exist as the lowest cost product, engineered wood and fiber cement deliver a better overall value proposition, primarily because of higher resistance (Vinyl is prone to cracking, especially in cold weather), better heat tolerance and longer warranty (LP SmartSide often comes with a 50-year limited warranty).

As a result, the market share of wood and vinyl structurally declined since the 2000s, while the market shares of fiber cement and engineered wood increased, as you can see below:

It is important to note that this graphic does not include engineered wood, which has grown even faster than fiber cement:

Within engineered wood and fiber cement, Louisiana-Pacific (engineered wood) and James Hardy (fiber cement) are clearly the dominant suppliers, with market shares of > 85% and > 90% in their respective markets in North America.

Both companies have been the subject of fierce competition for several years as to who can secure the largest share of the pie from wood and vinyl. The leadership of both companies even goes so far that some industry experts are drawing comparisons to Coke and Pepsi in the battle between LPX and JH. Either you're there for team Hardie, or for team Louisiana.

Between the two companies, I believe LPX's products offer structural advantages over JHX's as Louisiana's products are easier to handle and cut (thus cheaper and easier to install) and less prone to chips and breaks. Also, engineered wood siding is more environmentally friendly, but that's another discussion. While JHX are better in some areas due to superior fire resistance and insect resistance, this is only useful in some smaller markets.

Accordingly, Louisiana's volume growth has clearly outperformed that of James Hardy since 2015:

Given Louisiana-Pacific's product strengths, I expect that the company will continue to gain market share from vinyl, wood and fiber cement.

In my opinion, the possibility of further penetration still exists for many years to come. With a total market size still estimated at $15 billion, the siding segment of Louisiana captures only 10% of the total market based on total revenues of ca. $1.5 billion. The competing products hold a market share of:

  • 25% for vinyl, or ca. $3.75 billion
  • 5% for wood, or ca. $750 million
  • 22% for fiber cement, or ca. $3.3 billion.

This puts the incremental revenue potential for Louisiana-Pacific's siding products at almost $8 billion, or >5x today's revenues, assuming the market does not grow in the future.

The business also continues to benefit from:

  • A growing R&R market, supported by the aging of the North American housing stock, which means that the overall market will face structural tailwinds in the coming years
  • Penetration of new products such as ExpertFinish (ca. 13% of total sales) and BuilderSeries, which should raise the margin profile in the long term, as these products are both higher priced and have higher margins than existing products.

The current EBITDA margin of 25% should therefore increase over time.

How about competition? It's very unlikely that other OSB producers will come up with the idea of entering the market. According to statements made by the two largest competitors, West Fraser and Weyerhaeuser in personal conversations, the main reason is the lack of expertise, as siding involves a complicated technology. In addition, there are opportunity costs and project risks (including brand building). The same factors also apply to non-OSB competitors.

To conclude, the siding business is a great business with a high ROIC, high pricing power and a high moat that can still grow EBIT conservatively in the high single digits to low-teens range for many years to come resulting from the:

  • Aforementioned annual mid-single digit price increases
  • Expected mid single digit to high single digit volume increases
  • Shift to higher margin products.

But what is the value of the business?

Valuation Assumptions

I will value the siding segment based on a DCF model using a three-case scenario. Currently, the business generates ca. $240 million in Free Cashflow.

Regarding the growth value in the "best case" scenario, I use a 12% CAGR in the first two years and assume that growth will slow down over the years. I consider the growth rate to be very reasonable due to the factors mentioned above. In the "worst case" scenario, I use a CAGR of 8%. This is based on the assumption that I believe the business is capable of high single-digit growth even in a worst-case scenario due to its structural advantages and TAM opportunity.

Last but not least, I use a FCF multiple of 15x in the "best case" and 13x in the "worst case". Since usually companies with the corresponding growth rates will be valued with even higher multiples, I consider this to be reasonable. This leads to the following valuation:

Thus, the value of the Siding segment represents ca. 82% of the current market cap of ca. $6.4 billion.

OSB

OSB is the cyclical part of the business. OSB is the second-largest segment of Louisiana-Pacific, accounting for around 35% of sales, and comprises LPX's North American OSB business. The business is not very attractive by nature, as OSB is a very cyclical commodity product. Since OSB is primarily installed in new construction, selling prices are very sensitive to U.S. housing starts, which are known to vary widely across different stages of the economy.

Historically, the industry has been dominated by classic boom and bust cycles, where producers generate high profits in times of high demand and undersupply, and in some years record very high losses in times of low demand or oversupply.

Historical results of this segment are shown below:

In good times, such as the last five years, Louisiana-Pacific achieved EBITDA margins of up to 64%, while in bad times, such as the 2008 financial crisis, losses of up to $200 million had to be booked. The normalized EBITDA is around $300 million.

Important market developments in recent years have been that:

  • The market itself has developed into an oligopoly – the largest four players account for 70% of the market, compared to < 50% in 2005
  • Companies largely seem to act rational – they have learned from the past and have made almost no effort to increase the total market production capacity during the last peak
  • Louisiana-Pacific shifted its business to a higher share of Structural Solutions - a value-added OSB product that currently has a > 50% revenue share within the OSB segment, which is expected to increase to 75% long-term, vs. 25% during the financial crisis.

Overall, this has led to down cycles becoming less painful, as the chart below clearly shows:

On the upside, less capacity and less competition means quicker price hikes, higher profits and longer lasting peaks. According to Brad Southern, the CEO of LPX, the capacity of the industry today is only designed for 1.5-1.7 million new housing starts, while it was designed for 2.2 million housing starts at the time of the financial crisis. West Fraser even believes that supply shortages can be reached with as little as 1.4-1.5 million new housing starts. In contrast, the long-term historical average for new housing starts is around 1.4-1.5 million, while over the past decade, it has been significantly lower, partly due to the overheating pre-GFC. Currently, new housing starts in May were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of around 1.25 million, way below the historical average.

So, the bull case for this segment is that in the medium term we'll get back to a situation where houses reach the historical average (undersupply can't last forever), resulting in abnormal cash flows for the entire industry. If you look a few years into the future, despite near-term headwinds, it is very likely that this point will be reached again in the medium term, driven by:

  • Undersupply
  • Population growth.

All in all, the OSB industry is basically an oligopoly with high upside potential in good times, as well as less painful and shorter downside cycles in bad times. So how can the business be valued?

Valuation Assumptions

In my SOPT valuation of the OSB segment, I will use the peak FCF based on the historical results of the segment and apply an appropriate multiple to it. Looking at the annual reports from 2005-2024, the OSB segment stand-alone has been able to generate around $300-$700 million in FCF at peak times. I will use $500 million for the SOPT valuation, as this represents the average peak FCF. Additionally, I will use a peak FCF multiple of 3x in the "worst case" scenario and a multiple of 4x in the "best case" scenario. This should be appropriate due to the high cyclicality of the business. Based on the inputs above, I obtain the following fair value for the OSB segment:

This should be a very conservative valuation considering potential future tailwinds and the fact that the business is perfectly capable of generating over $800 million of FCF in very good years that are increasingly likely going forward.

South America

This segment is rather insignificant, with a total revenue share of less than 10%. Based on conservative assumptions, I estimate the value of the business to be around $240-$300 million with a FCF result of around $30 million in FY 2024 and using a FCF-multiple of 8-10x:

Louisiana-Pacific SOTP Valuation

If we put all the parts together, we get a SOTP valuation of $5.8-$8.7 billion for the whole business:

Thus, I consider Louisiana-Pacific to be fairly valued.

Risks

New housing starts continue to fall (perhaps even to <1 million levels) and remain at low levels for an extended period of time. This would invalidate my thesis, at least in the short term, but would not change the attractiveness of the business in the long term. The OSB segment has historically been able to operate profitably with as little as 800k restarts. So, even in this case, the company should be able to remain profitable after responding to changes in price and demand.

A competitor is launching a product that will disrupt LPX's siding products or OSB products. I think this point needs to be followed closely. I consider the risk in both segments to be possible, although not very likely in the medium term (especially in OSB as the product has been the standard for the last 50 years). However, this would definitely invalidate my thesis!

Other OSB producers are entering the siding market with competitive engineered wood products. This could pose a greater risk. Even if the barriers to entry are extremely high and it's very unlikely, this nonetheless needs to be monitored.

And finally, an overestimation of the siding segment. If the siding segment turns out to be less attractive than expected, this also invalidates my thesis.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Louisiana-Pacific is currently fairly priced by the market.

However, due to the high quality of the siding business, which offers a very good downside protection, as well as the high quality of the management, I remain invested and will be happy to take advantage of price drops as an opportunity to buy more shares of the company.

data.title: My Biggest New Investment For H2 2025: Blue Owl Capital
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola for High Yield Investor.

In early 2025, I explained that my biggest investment for the year was Patria Investments (PAX). The alternative asset manager was experiencing rapid growth, yet it was offered at a very low valuation relative to its close peers.

Since then, its stock has already surged 27% and significantly outperformed its close peers:

I am bringing this up because I think that Blue Owl Capital Inc. (NYSE:OWL) is today offering a similar opportunity.

These are not empty words, as I have bought so much of it lately that it has become my biggest new investment for H2 2025.

What Is Blue Owl Capital?

Just like Patria, it is an alternative asset manager that earns fee income in exchange for managing investments on behalf of other investors.

The biggest company in this space is Blackstone (BX), and it has been very rewarding to its investors over the long run, as it was able to scale its assets under management, and the fee income that comes with it:

What makes such businesses so rewarding is that they are capital-light, meaning they can grow at a rapid pace without requiring much of their own capital, and they earn highly recurrent fee income from their management services.

If you can convince investors to let you manage their investments, this can then turn into a very lucrative business, and I think that Blue Owl is on the cusp of such rapid growth.

It has guided to more than double its assets under management ("AUM") by 2029, and this is expected to result in 20%+ annual growth in its fee-related income:

Moreover, we believe that this guidance is conservative, as this management has a history of underpromising and overdelivering. It has actually grown a lot faster than this since going public, and it focuses on high-growth investment verticals that are today attracting a lot of capital.

This includes direct lending, GP minority stakes, and data centers.

Blue Owl is a global leader in all of these asset classes. Here is how fast it has grown since its IPO:

And here is why I like its stock so much:

Assuming no multiple expansion, this targeted growth has the potential to 2.5x its share price in five years.

But add to that the near 5% dividend yield and some multiple expansion, and you could reach a 3–4x in just five years.

The company has a slide about this on their investor deck:

And we have good reasons to believe that some multiple expansion is warranted here, given that Blue Owl currently trades at a discount to its peers, despite enjoying many qualities that warrant a premium valuation:

1) Safer cash flow: Blue Owl manages nearly exclusively permanent capital, and it derives its revenue from management fees, rather than incentive fees. The management has previously said that "permanent capital means we keep the AUM we have already raised – unlike peers, we don’t have to raise $2 to grow AUM by $1." It is a huge advantage. It also results in 3x more fees on average for each $1 of capital raised.

2) Faster growth: In addition to that, Blue Owl's biggest strategies enjoy significant growth tailwinds. Most notably, private credit is exploding in popularity, and there is enormous demand for data center investments. Blue Owl holds high exposure to these rapidly growing sectors, while many of its peers, like Brookfield (BAM), are more heavily invested in traditional sectors like office buildings and malls.

3) Stronger balance sheet: It has less leverage and more liquidity than many of its peers, and this is especially true when you take into account the fact that its cash flow is more resilient and growing at a faster pace. Unlike some of its peers, which have funded significant co-investments with their own balance sheets, Blue Owl has maintained a capital-light model with low leverage. It has a BBB investment grade rating and no major debt maturities until 2031.

4) Superior management alignment and track record: The management owns 36% of the equity, and they are very friendly to shareholders, focusing on growth on a per-share basis, avoiding dilutive equity raises, and focusing on growing the dividend and achieving strong total returns. Their track record speaks louder than words, as they have massively outperformed their close peers on all fronts since going public:

So, Why Is It Cheaper Today?

Just like Patria, I think that it is because the company went public just a few years ago, and it is still overlooked by most investors.

The listing also served as a liquidity event to some of its legacy owners, and this has led to some selling pressure that may have prevented it from reaching a higher multiple so far.

Finally, many of its large peers are part of the S&P 500 (SPY) and this has surely helped them reach a higher multiple, whether it is justified or not.

The good news is that Blue Owl is now eligible to be included in the S&P 500, and a future inclusion could serve as a strong catalyst for the stock:

Risks

The biggest risk would be a major credit event similar to the Great Financial Crisis. A dysfunctional financial system could then lead to a surge in default rates on its lending business, impacting its earnings and its market sentiment.

Blue Owl has a great track record in this space and should do fine in a regular recession, but it could suffer a major setback if we faced a real financial crisis.

Private lending can be rewarding, but you don't get those higher interest rates without taking some risks.

Closing Note

Ultimately, even without any multiple expansion, Blue Owl is well-positioned to generate strong returns from its 20 %+ expected annual growth rate and its near 5% dividend yield.

Barring any major bad news, multiple contraction seems unlikely, but future multiple expansion seems like a strong possibility and would only add to the upside.

The resulting risk-to-reward is very attractive, and this is why I have built a large position in the company.

data.title: Rare (Financial) Independence Day Specials: Very Undervalued Blue-Chip 10%+ Yields
data.description:
To my fellow American readers: Happy Independence Day! While we celebrate the blessings we enjoy thanks to the hard-won independence that our founding fathers earned for us, I wanted to share with you two compelling opportunities to advance your financial independence through dividend passive income.

It is fairly rare to find blue-chip stocks trading at attractive valuations, especially during a time when the broader markets are feeling a lot of optimism, as they are right now. It is even rarer to find a blue-chip stock that is undervalued and offers a double-digit dividend yield that should be sustainable for the foreseeable future. Yet, right now Mr. Market is offering a number of these opportunities, and in today's article I am going to discuss two in particular that look attractively priced at the moment.

Rare Buying Opportunity #1

The first one I will talk to you about today is Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL). MSDL matches virtually all the criteria for being a blue-chip BDC (BIZD), in that it is managed by one of the leading banks and asset managers in the world, Morgan Stanley (MS), which has strong shareholder alignment given that it has an 11.1% equity stake in MSDL.

Additionally, MSDL has a track record of steadily growing its base dividend payout—up by 25% between when it first started in 2020 as a private BDC to today's payout. On top of that, it has one of the most attractive fee structures in the BDC space among externally managed publicly traded entities. It also has one of the most defensively postured investment portfolios, with 98% of the portfolio invested in senior secured loans and 96.3% of the total portfolio invested in first-lien senior secured loans, as well as 95% of the portfolio invested in non-cyclical industries.

The portfolio is very well diversified across 210 borrowers and 34 industries as well, and the loans are fairly conservatively structured, with just a 40% weighted average loan-to-value on them. This conservative underwriting, as well as MS’s quality as an external manager, is evidenced by the very low non-accrual rate in the portfolio of just 0.2% at cost, giving it one of the best-performing portfolios at the moment in the entire industry.

On top of its quality and defensively postured portfolio, MSDL has an investment-grade balance sheet with a conservative net debt-to-equity ratio of just 1.07 times, which gives it substantial additional leverage capacity to reach the mid-range of its target ratio between 1.0 times and 1.25 times of leverage. Additionally, it has very little debt maturing in the near future, and even the debt that is maturing does so in September of this year at a 7.55% interest rate. This means that refinancing it at a much lower rate will not be a problem, which means it will actually be a tailwind to the company's net investment income (NII) per share.

When you combine that tailwind with the potential additional NII per share tailwind of adding a little bit more leverage to reach their target range, it means that MSDL should be able to add considerable additional earnings power moving forward. This is important because in the most recent quarter, net investment income per share came in at $0.52, which only covered the base dividend with a 4% cushion. However, with the Fed appearing in no rush to cut rates aggressively and these additional NII per share tailwinds, MSDL's dividend payout should be quite sustainable for the foreseeable future.

When you combine all of that with the fact that it currently trades at a 7.3% discount to its net asset value—despite very similarly structured peer Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), which has a similar fee structure and portfolio construction as well as underwriting performance—commanding a very substantial 14% premium to net asset value, MSDL looks quite attractively priced right now. This makes it a 10.44% yield backed by a blue-chip investment portfolio, underwriting, and balance sheet quality that is a rare, materially undervalued buying opportunity.

Rare Buying Opportunity #2

Another very attractive 10%+ yield right now is the Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC). While Kayne Anderson is not quite as renowned as Morgan Stanley (MS) is, it still has had some success with the relatively popular Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund (KYN), proving itself to be a reasonably skilled fund manager. However, what makes KBDC particularly attractive right now is that, like MSDL, it has a very defensively positioned portfolio with 98% of its investments in senior-secured first-lien loans.

It also has one of the most conservatively postured balance sheets in the BDC space, with a 0.86 times debt-to-equity ratio at quarter end. This means it has enormous additional capacity to get to its 1.0 to 1.25 times leverage target in the coming quarters, which means that it should enjoy a very powerful NII per share tailwind. This is important because its NII this past quarter was $0.40 per share, which is exactly in line with its regular dividend. However, the uplift from this significant additional leverage that it can add should enable it to easily cover its dividend payouts moving forward.

On top of that, its portfolio is pretty well diversified across 116 companies and 30 industries, and it will only get better diversified in the coming quarters as it adds additional investments by increasing its leverage ratio. Moreover, its counterparties are pretty high quality, given that they are 99% private equity-sponsored companies as there is a 4.2 times weighted average net leverage ratio on them, which is fairly reasonable, and 100% of them have financial covenants. There is also a 3.2 times weighted average interest coverage ratio on its loans.

Meanwhile, on a fair value basis, non-accruals are at 1.6%, which is higher than MSDL’s but still quite low compared to many peers in the industry. On top of that, the company’s balance sheet is in strong shape. It has no debt maturing until 2027, and that debt is currently at an 8.65% fixed interest rate. Its debt maturing in 2028 is at an 8.74% fixed interest rate. Given where interest rates are today, it is highly likely that they will be able to refinance that maturing debt in both cases at materially lower interest rates. This means that rather than facing a headwind from interest rates as it refinances its debt, it actually should be enjoying a tailwind.

This contrasts sharply with peers like BXSL, Ares Capital (ARCC), and others that are facing headwinds from having to refinance maturing fixed-rate debt at materially higher interest rates in the coming quarters. When you combine all that with the fact that it also trades at a 6.2% discount to its net asset value, KBDC looks like another highly compelling 10.33% dividend deal that should be sustainable for the foreseeable future. Additionally, it offers material capital appreciation potential through both an expanding price-to-NAV ratio as well as the potential for NAV per share growth as its NII is expected to be above its dividend in the coming quarters.

Investor Takeaway

Between MSDL and KBDC, investors get access to defensively positioned, well-managed portfolios of senior-secured investments at material discounts to net asset value. Additionally, these are backed by strong balance sheets with no headwinds from having to refinance debt at higher interest rates for the foreseeable future, as well as double-digit dividend yields that should be very sustainable moving forward.

Additionally, with their focus on floating rate loans, they can provide valuable diversification benefits for a high-yield portfolio invested in interest rate-sensitive securities such as REITs like Realty Income Corporation (O), utilities like NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE), and infrastructure companies like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. Limited Partnership Units (BIP). As a result, we hold positions in both of them in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: OPP: Don't Trust The Distribution As It Is Not Sustainable
data.description:
The RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund (OPP) is a closed-end fund that provides a method through which income-seeking investors can achieve their goals and earn a very high level of income from the assets that they possess. Historically, this fund has done a fairly good job at the provision of income, as is evidenced by its massive 13.33% current yield. This is substantially higher than the yield of just about any major fixed-income index, and fixed-income index funds are very commonly used choices for those investors who are seeking to earn a high level of income from their assets. This table summarizes the current yields of most of the index funds tracking the major domestic and global fixed-income indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.83%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.56%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.02%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (BNDX)

4.32%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.20%

As we can immediately see in this table, even relatively risky bonds such as domestic junk bonds or emerging market issues cannot compare to the yield offered by the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund. In fact, even if we were to double the yields on these two bond indices, we would not match the yield of this fund. There is something that certainly might make this particular fund appealing to many investors who are desperately in need of income. After all, the higher the yield of a particular asset, the more income that it will provide to its investors, assuming that an equal amount of money is invested in the asset compared to other options.

The RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund has also historically been a better option than most of the indices shown above. Over the past five years, the fund has delivered a 32.71% total return. The only one of the above indices that comes anywhere close to matching this performance over the trailing five-year period is domestic junk bonds:

Perhaps interestingly, we can see that the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index and both Vanguard index funds delivered negative total returns over the period. This is due to the fact that these three index exchange-traded funds include exposure to government and investment-grade corporate bonds. Many bonds issued by these entities have incredibly low yields due to the efforts that the Federal Reserve, European Central Bank, Bank of Japan, and other central banks expended in the years following the 2008 financial crisis to keep interest rates at very low levels. These efforts resulted in most investment-grade bonds having yields that are so low that the coupon payments were not sufficient to offset the decline that they suffered once interest rates started rising following the COVID-19 pandemic. This is one reason why I, and some other analysts in the alternative financial space, have stated that bonds have failed in their core role as a store of value. This seems unlikely to change going forward, as we are now beginning to see international entities expressing concerns about the U.S. dollar itself (see here). Fortunately, though, the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund did still manage to provide investors with a positive total return over the period, so that is something that potential investors in this fund might appreciate.

Peer Comparison in Terms of Distributions

The RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund also compares reasonably well with its peers in terms of yield. We can see that here:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund

Tactical Allocation

13.33%

AllianceBernstein Global High Income Fund (AWF)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

7.06%

BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund (HYT)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

9.52%

Invesco High Income Trust II (VLT)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

10.53%

KKR Income Opportunities Fund (KIO)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

11.61%

Neuberger Berman High Yield Strategies Fund (NHS)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

14.31%

PGIM Global High Yield Fund (GHY)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

9.25%

Pioneer High Income Fund (PHT)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

8.12%

Western Asset High Income II (HIX)

Fixed Income – Taxable – High Yield

13.52%

As we can see, the only two funds that manage to match the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund in terms of yield are the Neuberger Berman High Yield Strategies Fund and the Western Asset High Income Fund II. All of the other funds have lower yields, which adds support to my previous statement that this fund compares quite well with its peers in terms of the provision of income, and investors in it can expect to earn more income with this fund than they would with one of its peers.

Please note that Morningstar’s website states that the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund is a tactical allocation fund. However, the above table compares this fund to junk bond funds. There are a few reasons for this, and they are not mistakes on my part. First, as we can see here, the overwhelming majority of the fund’s holdings right now consist of various forms of debt securities:

We can see here that 72% of the fund’s holdings are in the Opportunistic Income Strategy segment, which is managed by DoubleLine Capital. In addition, 20% of the fund’s holdings are in the Alternative Credit Strategy segment, which is managed by RiverNorth. As I stated in my previous article on this fund, the Opportunistic Income Strategy segment consists primarily of non-agency mortgage-backed securities, commercial mortgage-backed paper, other asset-backed securities, and similar things. The Alternative Credit Strategy segment, on the other hand, consists of direct loans that the fund has made to small businesses. The Opportunistic Income Strategy segment’s holdings, in particular, are pretty similar to what might be found in a typical junk bond fund. Thus, it is more appropriate to compare this fund with junk bond funds than it is to compare it with funds that invest in other closed-end funds, such as the Saba Capital Income & Opportunities Fund (BRW).

Secondly, other websites besides Morningstar classify the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund as a junk bond fund. For example, both CEF Connect and CEF Data define it as one. While these websites are not Morningstar, they are widely used by investors in closed-end funds to get information about the various funds on the market. In this case, I believe that these sites are correct in classifying this as a junk bond fund, as its holdings have been heavily weighted towards junk bonds for a few years now. It is certainly possible that the fund will change its allocation in the future, but for now, it more closely resembles a junk bond fund than a fund that primarily invests in different assets.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund was on August 19, 2024. There have been a number of changes in the market that likely affected this fund, and a few of them are not what we would really expect.

The first of these changes, and one of the more important ones, is that the Federal Reserve finally started cutting its benchmark federal funds. This was a widely anticipated move, and the fund was positioned for it. Indeed, I even titled the previous article “Positioned For Falling Interest Rates, But There Are Some Risks.” One of the things that I mentioned in that article, which was at odds with the predictions made by many other market analysts, is that the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cuts would not cause bond prices to rise. I was actually correct in that prediction, as both domestic investment-grade bonds and domestic junk bonds fell following the first interest rate cut. Over the period from September 1, 2024, through December 31, 2024, junk bonds fell by 0.48% while investment-grade bonds fell by 3.45%:

One reason for this is that the market began worrying that the interest rate cuts would cause a resurgence of inflation, and indeed they did, as inflation started to rise in the months following December. I offered an alternative explanation on the reasons for the decline in bond prices following the interest rate cuts back in early June. In short, the market may be worried about the potential for the Federal Reserve engaging in some sort of yield curve control that destroys the value of the U.S. dollar and results in the currency that the investor receives back when a bond matures being worth far less than the amount that was originally lent in terms of what it can actually purchase. The fact that bond prices have been falling (and gold rising) year-to-date strengthens this particular thesis. There are only two ways for a bond investor to ensure that currency devaluation does not negatively impact them over the life of the bond:

  • Demand a higher interest rate to ensure that the bond delivers a positive real return over its lifetime.
  • Require the face amount to be adjusted upward based on some index, such as the inflation rate or the price of gold. This is how TIPS securities generally work, although it is very rare for any investment-grade bond issued by an American entity to have such a face value adjustment.

Investors have, for the most part, chosen the first option, and as such, bond prices have fallen and yields have risen even though the central bank is in the midst of an interest-rate cutting cycle.

We can expect the weakness in bond prices to have had a negative impact on the net asset value and the share price of the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund. The stock market, on the other hand, has been reasonably strong since our last discussion on the fund. As we can see here, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) is up 11.04% since that article was published:

While there was a bit of weakness in the technology sector following the release of DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) generative artificial intelligence product, and even more market weakness following the implementation of tariffs on many of America’s largest trading partners, these were eventually overcome, and the market still remains higher than it did back in August 2024.

The strength of the equity markets might have helped to offset the bond weakness in the fund’s portfolio. This offset would have come from that proportion of the fund’s portfolio that invests in other closed-end funds. Many closed-end funds, after all, invest in equities, and these would have gone up as the equity market rose. Of course, fixed-income funds would have delivered a much weaker performance over the same period due to the weakness in bonds, so how well the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund would have been able to take advantage of this depends on exactly what it was holding in its portfolio over the period.

At the time of the previous article, the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund was heavily invested in bonds. This is still the case today, so it most likely was holding the bulk of its assets in debt securities over the period. Thus, any offsetting gains from the equity funds in the portfolio probably would have been overshadowed by bond weakness and caused the fund to not perform as well as might be hoped.

This assumption proves to be correct, as shares of the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund are down 4.68% since our last discussion on the fund:

The fund’s performance trailed that of both the domestic equity index and both domestic investment-grade and domestic junk bonds. This is almost certainly going to be discouraging to any investor who might potentially be interested in this fund. However, it is not completely out of line with the fund’s portfolio. In fact, as we can see here, the fund’s net asset value is actually down 6.18% over the same period:

This means that the fund failed to fully cover all of the distributions that it paid out over the period, which is a bad sign. This suggests that the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund may need to cut its payout at some point in order to avoid running out of money. It also means that the fund’s shares actually performed better than the fund’s underlying portfolio. Thus, the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund is somewhat more expensive today than it was back in August of last year. Potential buyers should keep this in mind.

The RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund managed to do better on a total return basis, delivering 6.74% since our last discussion:

This was better than what investment-grade bonds delivered, but it was still not especially attractive. After all, we can see that both the S&P 500 (SP500TR) and the domestic junk bond index managed to deliver better performances. The fund still outperformed domestic investment-grade bonds, though, so at least that is something.

About The Fund

The fund’s website states that the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of current income and total return. This objective makes a great deal of sense given the fund’s strategy, as the fund invests in assets that both provide income and capital appreciation. For the most part, the bonds and debt securities that comprise the bulk of the fund’s portfolio deliver their investment returns in the form of current income. The fund does have some closed-end funds included in the “Tactical CEF Income Strategy” that comprises 8% of its total assets, and these might be able to deliver both current income to the fund through the distributions that they pay out, and distributions as well as capital gains as their share price increases. However, according to the fund’s first-quarter 2025 holdings report, nearly all of its holdings in other closed-end funds consist of their preferred stock as opposed to common stock. This document provides the following asset allocation:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Closed-End Funds – Common Shares

0.06%

Closed-End Funds – Preferred Shares

0.43%

Business Development Companies – Preferred Shares

2.09%

Bank Loans

4.29%

Small Business Loans

Collateralized Loan Obligations

10.86%

U.S. Government/Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities

U.S. Government Bonds and Notes

2.26%

Non-Agency Collateralized Mortgage Obligations

34.38%
