8.37%

The ability of most preferred stocks to deliver capital gains is very limited due to the fact that they do not have an inherent link to the growth and prosperity of the issuing companies. Preferred stocks are mostly just a security that pays a regular dividend to their owners (similar to a bond), but does not represent a debt obligation of the issuing company and does not cause the company to go into default if it fails to make the dividend payment. As is the case with bonds, preferred stocks rise in price whenever interest rates fall, and their performance does not necessarily depend on the profit of the issuing company. As interest rates cannot go below zero (or at least they cannot go very much below zero), the potential upside of preferred stocks is limited. Of the securities shown in the table above, the only things that have no theoretical limits on how high their price can go are the 0.06% allocation to the common stock of closed-end funds and the 0.00% common stocks in the portfolio. Neither one of these accounts for very much of the portfolio, so we can conclude that the ability of the fund’s profile to deliver a high level of capital gains is limited. The bulk of this fund’s investment profits will come from the regular payments that it receives from the debt and preferred stock. That is not necessarily a bad thing, but investors who are in a higher tax bracket may want to consider holding their shares of the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund in some sort of tax-advantaged account.

One thing that we immediately notice in the table above, as well as in the asset allocation chart that was provided earlier in the article, is that the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund appears to be more heavily invested in debt securities than it was the last time that we discussed it. Please recall that in the previous article, the fund had this allocation:

Portfolio Bucket

% of Total Assets

Opportunistic Income Strategy [DoubleLine]

68%

Alternative Credit Strategy [RiverNorth]

18%

Tactical CEF Income Strategy [RiverNorth]

14%

Today, the Tactical CEF Income Strategy bucket only contains 8% of the fund’s total assets. Thus, that particular segment of the fund’s portfolio decreased by 6% of its total assets. The remaining two segments, both of which invest in debt securities, increased proportionally, with the bulk of the increase found in the DoubleLine Capital-managed Opportunistic Income Strategy.

In the previous article on this fund, I made the following statement:

“I would prefer to see this fund have closer to a 50/50 split between the closed-end fund strategy and the credit strategy. This would allow it to earn the same or a higher level of income with a lower level of leverage.”

My opinion on this has not changed, and indeed, I am not at all happy about the fund’s exposure to debt rising at the expense of potential investments in other closed-end funds. One of the major trends year-to-date has been the market expressing a great deal of concern about the U.S. dollar going forward. The U.S. dollar index (DXY) is down 10.21% year-to-date:

In addition to this, U.S. Treasury yields at the long end of the curve (twenty-year and thirty-year) have actually been rising despite hopes of a near-term Federal Reserve rate cut. The only explanation for this is that the market believes that the U.S. dollar will be worth significantly less at the time that these bonds mature than it is today and is demanding additional interest to compensate for this. The Congressional Budget Office stated last month that the federal budget deficit for the first eight months of 2025 came in at $1.4 trillion, which was $160 billion higher than in the first eight months of the 2024 fiscal year. If the Federal government cannot reduce the deficits, then the Federal Reserve may have to step in and begin monetizing the debt. This will have a negative effect on the value of the U.S. dollar and pose problems for any bonds or other debt securities that are denominated in U.S. dollars. If the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund had a more balanced portfolio with a higher level of exposure to equities (and equity closed-end funds), then it would be better positioned to preserve the purchasing power of its portfolio. After all, a decline in the value of fiat currency relative to real assets is inflation by definition, and equities should be able to provide some protection against this because the revenue and profit of the issuing companies should rise as the price of their products rises. Debt securities offer no such protection, so the fund’s growing exposure to debt could be problematic.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of current income and total return. This fund primarily achieves this objective by making a regular monthly distribution to its investors. As of right now, this distribution is $0.0950 per share ($1.14 per share annually), which gives the fund a very impressive 13.33% yield at the current price.

This fund has, unfortunately, not been particularly reliable with respect to its distribution over its history. Over the past three years, the fund has cut its distribution three times:

We can see that the fund’s distribution went from $0.1478 per share to $0.0950 per share due to three cuts that occurred in early 2023, early 2024, and early 2025. This would appear to be an ongoing problem with this fund. Basically, the fund appears to be consistently failing to generate sufficient investment profits to cover its outsized distribution and then being forced to cut its payout. This is not something that most investors who are looking for a safe and stable source of income will want to see, but let us take a look at the fund’s finances to see if there is any chance that things will improve for it going forward.

As of the time of writing, the most recently released financial report for the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024. This report is admittedly half a year old at this point, which is disappointing, but most closed-end funds only release their financial statements on a six-month interval as opposed to quarterly, like many other entities. This is also a newer report than the one that we had available to us at the time of our last discussion, so it should still work pretty well for an update.

For the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund reported a net investment income of $12,556,791. This was not sufficient to cover the $13,908,157 that it paid out in distributions over the same period. I will admit, though, that this fund got closer to achieving full coverage of its distributions than I expected.

The fund failed to make up for the lack of sufficient net investment income through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund reported net realized gains of $1,018,285, but this was fully offset by $2,157,411 of net unrealized losses. Furthermore, the fund paid $2,737,536 to the holders of its preferred stock over the period. The fund’s operations, therefore, only produced $8,680,129 in cash:

That was not sufficient to cover the distributions, and the distributions that the fund paid out ended up being destructive to its net asset value. This explains why the fund’s management saw fit to cut its distribution back at the start of this year. However, even today’s smaller distribution is apparently not sufficient to fully cover the payout, as the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund has seen its net asset value decline by 1.65% since the start of this year:

Overall, then, there appears to be a risk that this fund will be forced to cut its distribution again if it cannot correct this problem in the near term. Clearly, there is a problem here for anyone who is hoping to earn some money that they can spend, as the declining net asset value means that an investor must use some of the distributions to purchase more shares of the fund to avoid having the value of their position go down.

Valuation

Shares of the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund are currently trading at a 4.13% discount to net asset value. This is mostly in line with the 4.93% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

However, historically, this fund has had a better price than it does today. Over the past three years, shares of the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 17.59% discount on net asset value to a 5.81% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period has been a 9.29% discount on net asset value, which is a far more attractive price than the one that this fund has today.

Here is how the price of the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund compares with that of its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund

(4.13%)

AllianceBernstein Global High Income Fund

(2.80%)

BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund

(0.82%)

Invesco High Income Trust II

(1.87%)

KKR Income Opportunities Fund

(2.64%)

Neuberger Berman High Yield Strategies Fund

(0.39%)

PGIM Global High Yield Fund

3.57%

Pioneer High Income Fund

(2.63%)

Western Asset High Income II

(0.69%)

With the notable exception of the PGIM Global High Yield Fund, all of these funds are trading at prices that are below their liquidation value. This is a good thing because it means that anyone purchasing the funds today will receive assets that are worth more than the purchase price of the shares. In addition, the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund is the most attractively priced out of its peer group today. Thus, the fund certainly does not appear to be very expensive when compared to the other funds in its peer group. However, it is still somewhat expensive when compared to its own historical price, so it might be possible to get a better price by waiting for the discount to expand to a level that is somewhat closer to its historical average.

Expenses

The fund’s semi-annual report states that the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund has an expense ratio of 2.67%. The same report also states that 0.59% of the fund’s reported expense ratio comes from the interest that it has to pay on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If we were to exclude the interest expenses, the fund’s expense ratio drops to 2.08%.

Please note that, for some reason, CEF Data claims that the fund has an expense ratio of 5.23%. It is unclear where that figure comes from, as it is substantially higher than the figure stated in the semi-annual report. It is possible that CEF Data annualized the figure provided in the report, but the financial report states that the 2.67% figure is annualized. Thus, it appears that the expense ratio provided on third-party sites for this fund is incorrect.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund is an incredibly high-yielding fund that income-seeking investors might consider purchasing as a method of achieving their goals. However, this fund’s ability to sustain its distribution is questionable, as it appears to be consistently failing to cover it and has to keep cutting. The fund’s share price performance has been disappointing as a result, and it is necessary to keep reinvesting some of the distributions in order to keep the size of the position stable. Thus, the portion of the distribution that an investor would be able to actually spend is lower than what might otherwise be assumed.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AeroVironment: Do Not Buy This Drone And Defense Tech Stock
data.description:
AeroVironment (NASDAQ:AVAV) stock has surged 27% since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 6% gain, despite tumbling after announcing a $1.47 billion capital raise through shares and convertible notes. In my previous report, I rated the stock a Hold due to its elevated trading multiple. Since then, the company has delivered strong returns and solid earnings, prompting a fresh look at the valuation and growth prospects. In this report, I analyze the recent capital raise, review the FY 2025 earnings performance, the outlook for FY 2026, and assess whether AeroVironment’s robust momentum now supports an upgrade to a Buy rating.

AeroVironment Raises Nearly $1.5 Billion

In May, AeroVironment completed the acquisition of BlueHalo, and I view the most recent capital increase in light of that acquisition. The acquisition itself was financed through shares. BlueHalo had $770 million in gross debt that AeroVironment aimed to refinance through a new term loan and cash on hands. The company has issued 3.52 million shares at $248 raising $875 million plus $650 million in 0% convertible notes maturing in 2030 with a conversion price equating to $322.40. The $1.47 billion in net proceeds will be used to reduce $965.3 million in indebtedness under the term loan and revolving credit facility, with the remainder being used to increase manufacturing capacity. In some way, it is a bit of a double blow for investors, as they first got diluted for the acquisition of BlueHalo and after that to service the debt load of BlueHalo. That also means that BlueHalo has to provide a lot of value to the company in investors to offset the dilution. There are options for another $131.25 million in stock to be issued and $97.5 million in convertible notes.

A Year With Double Digit Earnings Growth For AeroVironment

The full-year results showed a 14% increase in sales to $820.6 million driven by loitering munition sales while gross profit increased 12%. Lower service margins led to a slight erosion in gross profit growth compared to the revenue growth. EBITDA grew 15% $146.4 million while funded backlog increased 82% to $726.6 million. AeroVironment’s financial year ends in April, so the results do not yet include any contributions from BlueHalo and purely reflect the strength of the AeroVironment operational performance and product portfolio.

Analyst Expectations Exceed AeroVironment’s Guidance

For FY 2026, BlueHalo will contribute a full year to the results, leading to a revenue outlook of $1.9 billion to $2 billion, leaning a bit more towards the second half of the year. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to more than double to $300 million at the low end and $320 million at the high end, with 16% margins at the mid-point. Due to the dilution following the acquisition of BlueHalo, there will not be any growth in earnings per share. We do, however, note that on revenues analyst estimates have jumped from $1.15 billion to $2.01 billion while EPS of $3.11 are expected. While we see strong growth driven by the acquisition of BlueHalo, we do note that analysts' expectations are quite high compared to the guidance, so that is definitely a watch item.

Fundamental Upside Is Far Away For AeroVironment

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I processed the acquisition of BlueHalo, the capital increases and the debt refinancing, and it does not provide an extremely compelling investment case from a fundamental point of view. By 2027, I expect the leverage to be 4.5x, and we see no fundamental upside based on the elevated EV/EBITDA that AeroVironment historically trades at. Even with a 62.8% CAGR driven by the acquisition of BlueHalo and free cash flow turning from negative $24 million in FY 2025 to positive $150 million by FY 2027. The upside is really concentrated towards 2028 with around 31% upside and $321.87 price target. I usually let stocks trade a year ahead of earnings, which would provide a hold rating as the upside is only 1%. That brings us to a hold rating with a $321.87 price target for investors looking to hold the stock for several years.

AeroVironment Stock Prices More Driven By Drone Sentiment

I believe that with the war in Ukraine, investors have grown increasingly aware of the importance of drones and that boosts trading multiples for drone makers. I am a bit surprised by that, as the importance of drones is not new to me. So, while the market applies a premium valuation, I am less convinced of a proper justification of that premium. I also do note that drones are not an easy market to enter, but when it comes to entering the defense industry, drones provide without doubt the lower risk entry point. In that light, I see the AeroVironment valuation somewhat as a risk. At the same time, one could also make the point that the company is growing its drone and counter-drone solutions in an effort to stay a step ahead of emerging players.

Conclusion: AeroVironment Is A Better Hold

While I have little doubt that AeroVironment has a product portfolio that is expanding and in demand, as is also evident in its funded backlog, I believe the current valuation does not make for a strong investment case. Unless the company organically grows its business at rates above expectations, the stock price already reflects the BlueHalo contribution to earnings, making the current stock price a fair one projecting earnings almost two years out. So, if you are looking for a fundamentally attractive entry point, I can only conclude that at this point AeroVironment does not provide that. That is not to say that the stock will not see higher share prices because that could definitely happen on a bullish sentiment for investment in military drones, but fundamentally there is no strong buy case.

data.title: Fixed Income Outlook - Q3 2025
data.description:
Foreword

After a dizzying first half of the year, we take a step back to assess what has changed for markets, and just as importantly, what hasn’t.

The softening of tariff announcements has removed the worst-case scenarios from the growth and inflation outlooks. The effective tariff rate in the U.S. jumped from less than 3% to nearly 30% at the peak of reciprocal tariffs and has since settled in the mid-teens.1 Markets have rightly cheered this de-escalation, but with ultimate outcomes still unknown, it's crucial to monitor ongoing disruptions. Further, the real-world impact is starting to surface: import prices are rising and shipping container volumes are plunging as companies await clarity. While sentiment indicators have offered little insight in recent years, we still recognize that sustained low confidence and elevated inflation expectations can become self-fulfilling.

In the U.S., government spending cuts have entered the spotlight in recent weeks. Coupled with slowing immigration, the most immediate impact of these policies is likely to be felt in the labor market. Proposed federal funding cuts to Medicaid and universities could put real pressure on hiring in the education and health services sectors. Those sectors, combined with leisure and hospitality, have accounted for a massive 71% of all private employment growth in the last three years.2 As the new policies unfold, softer employment figures are a real possibility that could move the Federal Reserve to restart rate cuts.

It’s notable that there has been little sign of slowing growth in consumption data. Services consumption is the engine of U.S. GDP growth, which insulates the economy from global trade shocks relative to a manufacturing or export-heavy country. The bright spots in the first quarter’s distorted GDP numbers were consumption and investment: both display strong long-term tailwinds, largely independent of changes in trade. Households came into this episode in a uniquely strong position, with debt as a proportion of net worth at a seven-decade low, and wealth and income at highs. Of course, this is not an even distribution; lower-income households were, and still are, feeling the pinch of higher rates and inflation. Inflation had already stalled above target and has been trending higher in the past three and six months on an annualized basis. Continued stubbornness — or an outright acceleration — will further complicate the path for the U.S. Federal Reserve System (Fed).

The U.S. dollar’s (USD) status as the world’s reserve currency has come under renewed scrutiny amid the trade turmoil. The USD experienced its second-weakest two-month period since the financial crisis earlier this year — unlike the dollar’s strength through the tariff announcements in the first Trump administration year. For non-U.S. investors, this was a double blow — equity drawdowns of over 30% in some cases, amplified by currency losses. Notably, much of the selling occurred in overnight markets, suggesting a wave of foreign-led risk reduction. This marks the first time since 2002 that a weakening dollar has coincided with a major risk-off event, prompting institutions to question traditional correlations and reconsider reliance on U.S. assets.

That said, speculation about the dollar losing its reserve currency status remains far-fetched. No viable alternative currently meets the scale and systemic requirements to underpin global trade. The dollar taking a marginally less prominent share of foreign reserves, especially relative to the rising share of gold, is possible. Full-scale de- dollarization, if it ever comes, is still a long way away, but there is one dynamic playing out that could significantly raise that risk: increasing government debt.

We’ve been highlighting the precarious position of the U.S. government’s indebtedness for some time now, and, if left unchecked, we view debt as the single greatest risk to the “special status” of the U.S. in financial markets. The U.S. has the highest financing needs (as a percentage of GDP) among the G7, coupled with the shortest average debt maturity. Despite proposed spending cuts, deficits are still climbing — and more of that spending is now going toward interest payments. With foreign investors stepping back and the government issuing more than half a trillion dollars of debt weekly, the risk of private markets being unable to absorb this debt and consequently pushing government borrowing costs higher, is tangible.

In our first quarterly global Fixed Income Outlook, our colleagues across our Fixed Income platform focus on the market trends and opportunities for actively managed bond portfolios in Europe, and on the evolving utility of long-duration bonds as ballast for portfolios overall, drawing on a systematic fixed income lens for insight. We hope you enjoy and can profit from their thoughts, and we look forward to discussing these ideas with you in the second half of the year.

Income, income and income

Rick Rieder

Within large parts of the fixed income universe, duration is no longer the reliable hedge it once was. Given today’s macro backdrop, we don’t expect that to change. Still, with the Federal Reserve likely to keep rates above neutral for longer, debt markets are offering historically high income levels to investors once again (see chart). In this environment, we believe investors should prioritize income over duration. Yields across the board have rarely been this high, especially at the front-end, giving investors the chance to earn very attractive returns from high-quality borrowers with little duration risk. Even with inflation running higher than it has historically, these nominal yields dramatically eclipse run-rate inflation, something rarely seen over the prior few decades. And for the duration we do own in the U.S., we prefer to own in the front to belly of the curve. There, rates are unlikely to move higher and the Fed has more room to move in the event of any shock, making convexity increasingly attractive.

Over the last 20 years, how much time has each asset class been in each yield bucket?

With the potential to achieve consistently high levels of income, and with contained drawdowns, we believe the current fixed income landscape provides a generational opportunity for bond allocations, particularly given its income recognition and stability in this unstable world. Although we believe the U.S. is unlikely to be displaced as the global hub of innovation and the economy is positioned to remain resilient over the intermediate term, the short-term picture may be bumpy. In a sense, the ability to deliver consistently high coupons to global portfolios can serve as something of a bulwark against equity market drawdowns, especially at a time when duration itself has failed to serve its traditional function.

Markets have moved away from excessive expectations of US Federal Reserve System (Fed) cuts for the year. In our estimation, growth should slow from the first quarter’s robust readings even with tariff de-escalation, making the front-end a good core holding with return upside as well as beta-hedging characteristics. Meanwhile, the global rate-cutting cycle is likely to continue — and could even be accelerated — due to deflationary pressures from the rerouting of global trade in goods. In contrast to the U.S., lending opportunities in Europe and parts of Asia offer compelling ways to gain longer-duration exposure. In a market environment marked by heightened uncertainty and unreliable correlations, the case for a larger allocation to stable, income-generating investments has never been stronger.

Reallocation and regional expertise

Simon Blundell and James Turner

Tariff concerns have been the main drivers of performance across developed and emerging markets, and European fixed income is no exception. In addition, geopolitical uncertainty, including the rising tensions in the Middle East has further complicated the global investment landscape. These developments have intensified investor caution, underscoring the importance of maintaining well diversified portfolios to navigate an increasingly complex and volatile environment.

However, during the period of heightened volatility in April, there was a clear divergence between U.S. and European markets (in particular within government bonds), with German Bunds demonstrating their risk-hedging features in contrast to the moves observed by U.S. Treasuries (which hasn’t historically been the case!). This divergence was not only observed in risk-free assets, however. Fund flows show global investors starting to challenge the strength of the U.S. and diversify away from U.S. assets. That said, the starting point matters. We entered this year at an all-time high of non-U.S. investors holding U.S. investments.

As such, we expect this reallocation of capital to be a gradual shift that continues with certain key drivers of the global macroeconomic landscape pushing in that direction. One of those drivers is inflation expectations and what that means for monetary policy in the face of tariffs. Where are all these assets likely to go?

Change in 10Y rate since “Liberation Day”

Given the magnitude of global demand for U.S. assets, we don’t think there is a single market that could absorb even a modest reallocation out of the U.S. To illustrate this point, consider that U.S. investment-grade BBB assets are almost double the size of Germany’s entire sovereign debt market. That said, we do see a number of key beneficiaries across the globe, including Europe and parts of Asia, with the former offering stability and liquidity, and the latter better yields and growth.

The risk-averse nature of the largest investors in U.S. fixed income means that European fixed income is set to benefit more than Asian or emerging markets as stability is a key consideration. European liquidity is sufficient and is expected to improve further as a result of the increased issuance expected from governments in the region. This is especially true for Germany following the “ReARM” and “SAFE” programs announced by the EU and the new German government’s defence and infrastructure packages.

Positioning for what’s ahead

In a market characterized by higher rates and elevated volatility, the uncertainty from tariff negotiations, geopolitical tensions, and a U.S. fiscal conundrum warrant active investment.

Regional expertise from dedicated resources is going to be a crucial driver of performance, trumping global market themes. Access to a deep bench of local research analysts, traders and capital markets experts is crucial in bottom-up investment idea generation. Portfolio managers need flexibility and conviction to take targeted positions and focus on relative value opportunities as large directional bets will be less effective in this market environment.

We anticipate growing divergence between leaders and laggards and see plenty of opportunities in the European fixed income universe across macro and credit markets. One of them is the ultra-long EUR curve steepeners, where we expect upward pressure on the long end of the curve due to the normalising of the risk-free curve amidst the cutting cycle and medium-term regulatory changes coming from Dutch pension Fund reform. We are also seeking opportunities in sectors relatively insulated from tariffs, with bank debt remaining our preferred risk expression. European banks delivered solid results in the first quarter, affirming fiscal year 2025 guidance. However, some banks with large corporate loan books and U.S. consumer exposure took precautionary provisions or missed consensus, underscoring the importance of security selection.

Given the distinct impact political developments have in certain sectors and issuers, with greater divergence between winners and losers, we think that a dynamic active management approach offers the ability to adapt as market conditions evolve and new opportunities emerge.

You can run, but you can’t hedge: The bond market’s warning shot

Tom Parker and Jeffrey Rosenberg

James Carville once said that if he could be reincarnated, he’d want to come back as the bond market because “You can intimidate everybody.” That sentiment is echoing loudly in 2025.

Across both trade and, more recently, tax policy, the bond market warnings harken back to an era of “bond market vigilantism” that sparked Carville’s quip. During President Clinton’s first term, rapidly rising interest rates forced a shift in administration priorities away from spending and toward deficit reduction. And though Liz Truss’ U.K. government felt a similar sort of “bond market vigilantism” during the brief U.K. debt crisis in September 2022, U.S. policymakers have faced little market pushback to significant expansions in U.S. indebtedness over the course of the Global Financial Crisis and subsequently Covid-19.

But when "Liberation Day" tariffs were announced, negative market reaction in stocks was accompanied by surprising declines in the dollar and bonds (and for a short time, even gold). This is not the normal pattern for financial panics, since the dollar and U.S. Treasuries have traditionally been the ultimate safe-haven assets.

The recent U.S. tax bill deliberations have been met with concerns over the longer-run trajectory for debt and deficits. That corresponded with a not entirely unexpected downgrade of the U.S. credit rating by Moody’s, further highlighting concerns.

Is this a bond market warning shot? What we have been calling the “new conundrum” — namely, that longer-term interest rates can rise even as the Fed is expected to cut rates — is the opposite of the original (old) “conundrum” coined by then-Fed Chair Alan Greenspan in 1995 to describe the situation of falling long-term interest rates even as he was raising short-term rates.

The manifestation of a “new conundrum” stems from three key changes in the structural environment for bonds:

  1. Inflation certainty moving towards uncertainty: inflation no longer running persistently below target but now going on more than four years of inflation running persistently above target
  2. Rising Treasury supply: debt and deficit levels pushing up the supply of Treasury debt
  3. Falling Treasury demand: the end of the global quantitative easing era, a slowdown in recycling of dollar reserves by foreign central banks, and reduced hedging efficacy of long-maturity bonds are all contributing to weaker demand for Treasury debt

Hence, the direction of long-term rates may not follow Fed policy as strongly as it has in the past (or even at all). That means where you hold duration matters as much as how much duration you hold. And that means thinking more broadly about the diversification properties of traditional fixed income as well as beyond just long-end U.S. duration for portfolio hedges. We see more value for hedging efficacy in shorter maturities (when using leverage to equate the percentage impact on a portfolio) and global opportunities versus traditional long-end U.S. fixed income.

You can run, but you can’t hedge: The bond market’s warning shot

How a systematic lens can help navigate the fixed income landscape

Two inputs help to illustrate the opportunities. First, we use large language models (LLMs) to analyze sentiment readings on expectations for fiscal policy support across multiple countries. Zooming into just the differences between U.S. and European policy expectations in the charts below, we can see the more rapid increase in expansionary fiscal policy expectations in Germany corresponding to the shift in German fiscal policy this year versus a slower recovery in U.S. fiscal sentiment.

Systematically capturing policy sentiment and economic regime expectations

We can then compare this fiscal sentiment indicator with a measure of economic regime outcomes, here expressed as the difference between “hard landing” and “soft landing” regimes. These regimes capture the cross-sectional returns to characteristic portfolios mapped to different economic outcomes.

The pattern in both regions over the course of 2025 looks similar with “hard landing” outcomes surging post “Liberation Day” tariff announcements and then fully retracing. But the gap between them highlights the much less negative economic scenarios being priced into European markets vs. U.S. markets. That might reflect the more supportive fiscal policy stance in Europe.

Hard and soft landing mapped to different economic outcomes

For the U.S., returns to “hard landing” relative to “soft landing” scenarios have fully recouped earlier underperformance, suggesting that despite ongoing policy uncertainty, markets are no longer pricing the downside risk to economic growth. Meanwhile, for Europe, the cumulative returns to “soft landing” over “hard landing” imply a better economic expectation being reflected in prices. With bond market expectations for both the ECB and Fed pricing in around only two cuts for the remainder of the year, Fed cuts may be underpriced relative to the ECB.

Shaky markets, steady yields

Pat Haskell, Head of the Municipal Bond Group

U.S. municipal bonds (munis) are prized for their tax advantages, but their historic tendency to provide a stable source of return also makes them valuable amid market volatility and uncertainty.

Munis are generally less vulnerable to inflation shocks or the crossfire of global trade policies because they are often linked to public authorities that provide fee-based essential services, such as waste collection and public transportation, or secured by taxes on sales, property and income.

Munis have also shown historically low default rates and high credit ratings (Aa3 versus Ba1 for global corporate debt, on average) thanks to the disciplined finances and stable revenues of most state and local governments.

Tax equivalent yields of munis have reset to levels not seen consistently in over a decade — 6.79% and 9.86% for investment grade and high yield respectively.3 Against this backdrop, we see opportunity to increase allocations, particularly as the outlook for limited supply relative to demand in July and August could bolster performance.

Credit spotlight

In today’s market of higher yields and narrower spreads, we are cautious about sectors such as healthcare and higher education that are vulnerable to policy risks. Instead, we favor high-quality state and local government debt, which offers access to higher nominal yields and protection from credit volatility. Uncertainties abound, but the “America First” mantra has been clear and consistent, making munis that support U.S. infrastructure a well-aligned opportunity.

Possible antidote to tariffs and recession concerns

Amid Wall Street’s growing concern over a potential tariff-induced recession, investors are seeking refuge in areas least affected by global supply chains. This is fueling interest in state and local government bonds for the following reasons:

  • Limited exposure to trade risks. A broad-based economic slowdown would reduce state revenues, but, as a service-centered economy, we believe the U.S. is less vulnerable to trade volatility despite significant exposure to trade in automobiles, agriculture and petrochemicals. Among the 50 states, the median gross trade-to-GDP ratio with China — which poses the greatest tariff risk — is just 1.4%, with Tennessee having the highest ratio at 4.6%.4 Only a few states have total economic debt (debt + unfunded retirement benefits) to GDP ratios of more than 20%: this compares favorably to other sovereign and sub-sovereign debt issuers around the world.
  • Built-in Inflation buffers. States rely heavily on federal transfers to fund programs such as Medicaid, but their main funding sources are sales and income taxes. In fact, more than 70% of state General Fund revenues, which fund core state operations and state aid to local school districts, are derived from sales and personal income, linking them broadly to economic performance but not to specific trade actions or federal budget discussions.5 Because states depend more on broad economic activity than trade, state finances benefit from built-in inflation protections as tax revenues rise proportionally with wages and prices.
  • Rising local tax revenue. For local governments, property taxes — based on the assessed value of real estate — are the main source of revenue and national home prices have had steady annual gains, including 4.5% in 2024, appreciating each quarter since the start of 2012.6
  • Resilient state balance sheets. States are the largest issuers of municipal debt and possess sovereign powers that allow them to levy taxes and pass costs onto their municipalities. Unlike the federal government, states cannot run deficits through debt issuance. In fact, 46 of the 50 states have constitutional balanced budget requirements.7 Over the past decade, state balance sheets steadily improved due to increased reserves and reduced debt, providing resilience in the face of a potential economic slowdown.

Rainy day funds provide ample liquidity

Reserves as percent of General Fund spending, 1988-2024

Bottom line

We believe municipal bonds’ long-term record of stable return provides a unique opportunity to enhance portfolio resilience in what is likely to remain a less-than-stable macro environment. Their attractive yields relative to taxable counterparts and potential ballast to equity risk make munis particularly compelling, with a favorable supply/demand picture in the summer months setting the stage for strong performance.

Bonds without borders

Michael Gates, Head of Model Portfolio Solutions for the Americas

In the current environment, we are swayed by a desire to hedge against potential near-term tariff-driven inflation surprises while building in resilience against possible slower growth and more pronounced disinflation later in the year. Short-term Treasury Inflation Protected Securities (TIPS) play a role here.

Consistent with overall macro themes at play, one area where we have seen the opportunity to enhance the diversification characteristics in portfolios is with international bonds.

While the potential one-time inflationary shocks that will take place inside the U.S. (as the tariffing country) have been well covered, what is interesting to point out is that this will also result in one-time deflationary shocks for tariffed countries (i.e., the rest of the world). This should actually put more downward pressure on rates in regions outside of the U.S. and thus create potential upside price appreciation for non-U.S. bonds as these effects are realized.

We also see global central banks (like the European Central Bank, shown on the following slide) attempting to combat lower growth and acknowledge lower inflation by cutting interest rates more often and more aggressively than the Fed. This creates a near-term opportunity, in our view, to benefit tactically from non-U.S. government and investment grade bond exposure.

Importantly, we favor a USD-hedged approach to investing in non-U.S. bonds given the more attractive risk characteristics and additional carry the currency hedging brings.

Enhancing diversification with opportunities overseas

Policy rates of Fed vs. ECB

Performance of U.S. vs. international bonds since Fed's Sept rate cut

Sources: BlackRock, Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, Global Aggregate ex-U.S. 10% Issuer Cap Total Return Index Hedged, performance from September 24, 2024 to May 15, 2025. Federal Reserve fed funds rate and European Central Bank (ECB) deposit facility announcement rate, as of May 15, 2025. Index performance is for illustrative purposes only. Index performance does not reflect any management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

So how should this all fit in a whole portfolio of stocks and bonds? As portfolio constructors, we have to consider a few characteristics in an allocation like this, because the devil is often in the details of sizing these kinds of positions. Many fixed income benchmarks are home-biased, so an allocation to international bonds adds to active risk. The source of the position can also affect the overall duration and credit profile of the bond sleeve. This is significant because of the role of bonds as a diversifier to stocks. Generally, we have sized and sourced allocations to international bonds in a way that does not impact or limit the active risk we are taking in equities, where we currently see a more compelling risk-return opportunity.

1Source: Bureau of Economic Analysis and BLK Estimates, as of May 31, 2025.

2Source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics as of May 2, 2025.

3Source: Bloomberg, as of June 6, 2025. Tax-equivalent yields based on the yield to worst for the Bloomberg Municipal Investment Grade Index and Bloomberg Municipal High Yield Index and a 40.8% tax rate.

4Source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, as of December 31, 2024.

5Source: National Association of State Budget Officers Fall 2024 Fiscal Survey of States.

6Source: National Association of State Budget Officers as of June 30, 2024. Source:

data.title: Worried About Potential U.S. Stagflation? Consider IDVO
data.description:
Introduction

For the first 6 months of 2025, market returns were pretty stagnant. But due to alleged trade deal negotiations going well, the S&P (SP500) recently made new highs.

However, there's still much uncertainty from future deal fallouts, potential stagflation, and a slower economy in the months ahead. Last quarter we saw negative GDP for the first time in 3 years.

Additionally, the Federal Reserve recently downgraded their growth projections for GDP for the next two years, resulting in just 1.4% growth in 2025 and 1.6% in 2026.

And as a result of future global trade policies and potential stagflation, diversifying your portfolio outside the U.S. may not be a bad idea right now.

With slower projected growth, how will the U.S. stock market fare going forward? Will we see underwhelming returns?

While I think U.S.-based stocks will do fine over time, seeing double-digit returns like we did in 2023 and 2024 are unlikely.

In this article, I discuss why income-oriented investors should consider diversifying outside of U.S. equities, and why Amplify CWP International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF is one you should consider.

Overview

Amplify's CWP International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (NYSEARCA:IDVO) seeks to track the performance of the MSCI AC World Index Ex USA Net Index. The ETF went public on September 8, 2022, and looks to hold between 30 and 50 holdings at any given time.

They also seek to provide income & lower volatility by investing in quality, dividend-paying stocks with strong track records and selling covered call options. They look to pay out 3% - 4% from dividend income and 2% - 4% from writing call options.

IDVO screens companies based on earnings & cash flow growth, ROE (or return on equity), market cap, and management track records. They are actively managed, a reason for the higher than average expense ratio of 0.66%. However, this is still solid, as I prefer ETFs with an expense ratio of less than 1%.

Portfolio

IDVO's portfolio is diversified with 52 holdings to include top names like Philip Morris International (PM), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Novartis AG (NVS), and Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG) as some of the top names.

Their top sectors are Financials, Consumer Discretionary, and Materials as of last quarter.

And although they do invest in U.S.-based companies, their top countries include the United Kingdom, Canada, and Brazil. The U.S. currently makes up 6.5% of their portfolio.

Return Comparison

Although they have less than 3 years since inception, their returns over the past 2 years and 10 months have been impressive. For comparison, I pitted IDVO up against JPMorgan's (JPM) Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) and Schwab's (SCHW) International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY).

Both JEPQ and SCHY were formed before IDVO, with the former in May 2022, and SCHY in April 2021. So, for comparison, I use a start date a month after IDVO's inception.

While JEPQ slightly outperformed IDVO, the latter bested both SCHY and the S&P in total returns, returning a solid 70.38% in less than 3 years.

This Could Change Going Forward

Currently, the U.S. has the world's largest stock market, and this is likely to remain for many years to come. However, according to Goldman Sachs (GS) Research, emerging economies are expected to surpass the United States and grow faster than developed markets by 2056.

If so, this means international stocks could outperform U.S.-based equities in the years ahead. For a long time, international companies have underperformed the U.S. but over the next 30 years this could change. So, diversifying your portfolio outside the U.S. may be a good decision to see potential outperformance down the line.

Why Should You Invest?

Besides portfolio diversification, IDVO's dividend yield of 5.53% is also attractive. Although significant upside is capped due to their covered call strategy, investors can see some price appreciation while generating high income.

And despite underperforming peers SCHY and Vanguard's International High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYMI) in the past year in terms of price, IDVO's dividend yield was significantly higher than both's 3.86% and 4.17% respectively.

Stocks like Verizon (VZ) and Realty Income (O), both quality stocks and favorites amongst retirees, failed to outpace the rate of inflation with their dividend increases.

Verizon's annual dividend increase of 1.9% back in September to $0.6775 sits below the current inflation rate of 2.35%, while Realty Income's annual increase was slightly below at 2.3% from $0.263 to $0.269.

And although Verizon has a higher yield and Realty Income's is slightly lower at 5.52% compared to 5.53% for IDVO, the ETF's superior dividend growth year-over-year and covered call strategy makes them particularly attractive.

Perfect For Income-Oriented Retirees

Due to their call option strategy, Amplify's International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF's distributions are considered ordinary income, or capital gains. This makes them perfect for retirees over the age of 59 1/2 as you can hold these in a tax-advantaged account like a Roth IRA and collect your income without penalties.

And as previously mentioned, their yield above 5% and the fact that they pay monthly vs quarterly like SCHY and VYMI also makes them attractive if you rely heavily on income in retirement.

Since inception, IDVO has grown its distribution from $0.12 to their latest $0.169. This year so far, their distributions have averaged $0.1626 a share, providing investors with a reliable income stream. From 2023 to 2024, their annualized dividend grew a solid 10.9%.

Comparing this to JEPQ, SCHY, and VYMI, IDVO beat all three in terms of annual dividend growth.

ETF
